question: [File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: getBlocks()] Can an unprivileged external user send `timestamp=lt:1676540001.234490000` (a timestamp matching the exact `consensus_end` of an early block) to `getBlocks()` so that `extractSqlFromBlockFilters()` generates `WHERE consensus_end < 1676540001234490000`, excluding all blocks at or after that timestamp, and any consumer computing the latest block time from this response sees a timestamp far in the past, simulating a 500%+ block delay?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: extractSqlFromBlockFilters()] Can an unprivileged external user send the maximum number of repeated `timestamp` parameters all with `lt:` operators set to progressively smaller values, so that `extractSqlFromBlockFilters()` generates multiple `consensus_end < X` conditions that together exclude all blocks, causing the response to contain zero blocks and making block-time monitors record an infinite block delay?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: getBlocks()] Can an unprivileged external user send `order=asc&limit=1` with no timestamp or block.number filter to `getBlocks()`, causing `RecordFileService.getBlocks()` to return the oldest block in the database, and if a consumer uses this endpoint to determine the 'current' block time, the returned `timestamp.to` (consensus_end) would be far in the past, making the computed block delay appear to exceed 500% of the 24-hour average?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: generateNextLink()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `generateNextLink()` uses `last(blocks).index` as the pagination cursor (always `block.number` regardless of the original filter type), causing a consumer that originally filtered by `timestamp` to receive a next link with `block.number=gt:X`, which when followed returns blocks in a different order and skips blocks whose `index` is non-sequential, creating apparent gaps in block production timing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: getByHashOrNumber()] Can an unprivileged external user send a hash string of all zeros (`0x` + `0`*64) to `getByHashOrNumber()`, which passes `isValidEthHashOrHederaHash()` validation, causing `RecordFileService.getByHashOrNumber()` to execute `HASH like '000...0%'` and potentially match the genesis block with `prev_hash` of all zeros, returning a block with a `timestamp.to` from the network's inception that signals an extreme block delay?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: extractSqlFromBlockFilters()] Can an unprivileged external user send `block.number=lte:0` (block number 0 with lte operator, since `isPositiveLong(val, true)` allows zero) to `getBlocks()`, causing `extractSqlFromBlockFilters()` to generate `WHERE index <= 0`, returning only the genesis block with its original `consensus_end` timestamp, which when used by monitoring tools to compute block time produces a 500%+ delay relative to the current time?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: getBlocks()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with `limit=1` and `order=asc` and `block.number=gte:0` to `getBlocks()`, causing the API to return the very first block ever recorded, and if a downstream system uses the returned `timestamp.from` (consensus_start) as a reference point for computing average block time, the computed average would be skewed to make any recent block appear to have a 500%+ delay?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: extractLimitFromFilters()] Can an unprivileged external user send multiple `limit` parameters (e.g., `limit=1&limit=2&limit=3`) so that `findLast()` in `extractLimitFromFilters()` picks the last value, and by carefully choosing the last limit value to be exactly `maxLimit`, cause the DB to return the maximum number of rows per request, enabling a high-frequency flood of max-limit requests that saturates the DB and delays block data delivery?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: getByHashOrNumber()] Can an unprivileged external user send a path parameter that is a valid positive long integer equal to a block number that has a `consensus_end` timestamp significantly older than the current time (e.g., a block from 24+ hours ago), causing `BlockViewModel` to expose `timestamp.to` data that, when consumed by a block-time monitor, computes a delay exceeding 500% of the 24-hour average block time?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: getBlocks()] Can an unprivileged external user send `timestamp=gte:0.000000001` (near-zero timestamp) to `getBlocks()` so that `extractSqlFromBlockFilters()` generates `WHERE consensus_end >= 1`, returning all blocks from the beginning of time, and when combined with `order=asc&limit=1`, the response contains the oldest block whose `timestamp.to` is far in the past, misleading consumers about the current block production rate?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: extractSqlFromBlockFilters()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `extractSqlFromBlockFilters()` does not deduplicate or validate the logical consistency of multiple `block.number` and `timestamp` filters, sending contradictory conditions like `block.number=gte:1000&block.number=lte:999` that produce an impossible WHERE clause, causing the DB to return zero rows and making block-time monitors record a production freeze?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: generateNextLink()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request that returns exactly `filters.limit` blocks, causing `generateNextLink()` to always produce a non-null next link (since `blocks.length !== filters.limit` is false), and then use this next link in a rapid automated loop to generate thousands of sequential DB queries, exhausting the connection pool and delaying block data responses by 500%+ of the average block time?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: getBlocks()] Can an unprivileged external user send `block.number=gt:X` where X is the current latest block index, causing `RecordFileService.getBlocks()` to return zero blocks, and any monitoring system that polls this endpoint to detect new blocks interprets the empty response as the network having stalled, triggering false alerts about a 500%+ block time delay?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: validateHashOrNumber()] Can an unprivileged external user send a path parameter that is a hex string of exactly 96 characters without `0x` prefix (Hedera hash format) to `validateHashOrNumber()`, which passes `isValidEthHashOrHederaHash()` and calls `RecordFileService.getByHashOrNumber()` with `HASH like '<96chars>%'`, and if the hash is a valid prefix of a very old block's hash, the returned block's `timestamp.to` would indicate a 500%+ block delay?
